sections.title: Introduction to Inflammation
sections.content: Inflammation is a response of vascularized tissues to infections and damaged tissues that brings cells and molecules of host defense from the circulation to the sites where they are needed, in order to eliminate the offending agents. Although inflammation helps clear infections and other stimuli and it should be followed by repair, the inflammatory reaction and the subsequent repair process can also cause significant tissue damage. The main components of inflammation are a vascular reaction and a cellular response; both are activated by mediators that are derived from plasma proteins and various cells. The steps of the inflammatory response can be remembered as the five Rs: (1) recognition of the injurious agent, (2) recruitment of leukocytes, (3) removal of the agent, (4) regulation (control) of the response, and (5) resolution (repair).

sections.title: Acute Inflammation: Vascular Changes
sections.content: Acute inflammation has three major components: (1) dilation of small vessels leading to an increase in blood flow, (2) increased permeability of the microvasculature enabling plasma proteins and leukocytes to leave the circulation, and (3) emigration of the leukocytes from the microcirculation, their accumulation in the focus of injury, and their activation to eliminate the offending agent. Vasodilation is induced by the action of several mediators, notably histamine, on vascular smooth muscle. It is one of the earliest manifestations of acute inflammation and is sometimes preceded by a transient vasoconstriction. Increased vascular permeability is a hallmark of acute inflammation. Mechanisms include endothelial cell contraction leading to intercellular gaps in postcapillary venules (the most common mechanism, elicited by histamine, bradykinin, and leukotrienes), endothelial injury resulting in cell necrosis and detachment, and increased transcytosis of proteins.

sections.title: Leukocyte Recruitment to Sites of Inflammation
sections.content: The journey of leukocytes from the vessel lumen to the tissue is a multistep process mediated by adhesion molecules and cytokines. It is divided into: (1) marginalization, rolling, and adhesion to endothelium; (2) migration through the endothelium and vessel wall; and (3) movement in the tissues toward a chemotactic stimulus. In the lumen, as blood flow slows, leukocytes take a peripheral position along the endothelial surface (marginalization). Rolling is mediated by the selectin family of adhesion molecules (L-selectin on leukocytes, E-selectin on endothelium, and P-selectin on endothelium and platelets). Firm adhesion is mediated by integrins on leukocyte surfaces interacting with ligands on endothelial cells (e.g., ICAM-1, VCAM-1). Chemokines act on the adhering leukocytes and stimulate the cells to migrate through interendothelial spaces toward the chemical gradient (chemotaxis). Common chemotactic agents include bacterial products, cytokines (e.g., IL-8), components of the complement system (C5a), and arachidonic acid metabolites (e.g., LTB4).

sections.title: Chemical Mediators of Inflammation
sections.content: Chemical mediators of inflammation may be produced locally by cells at the site of inflammation, or may be derived from circulating plasma precursors. The most important cell-derived mediators are vasoactive amines (histamine, serotonin), arachidonic acid metabolites (prostaglandins, leukotrienes, lipoxins), platelet-activating factor (PAF), cytokines (TNF, IL-1, IL-6, chemokines), and reactive oxygen species and nitrogen species. Plasma-derived mediators include complement proteins, kinins (bradykinin), and coagulation factors. Histamine causes vasodilation and increased vascular permeability, and is the principal mediator of the early vascular response in acute inflammation. Leukotrienes (especially LTB4) are powerful chemotactic agents. Prostaglandins are responsible for pain and fever. TNF and IL-1 play pivotal roles in systemic effects of inflammation including fever, acute phase response, and leukocytosis.

sections.title: Chronic Inflammation
sections.content: Chronic inflammation is inflammation of prolonged duration (weeks to years) in which active inflammation, tissue injury, and healing all proceed simultaneously. Chronic inflammation may follow acute inflammation, or begin insidiously as a low-grade, smoldering response without any manifestations of acute inflammation. Chronic inflammation is characterized by: (1) infiltration with mononuclear cells (macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells); (2) tissue destruction, largely induced by the products of the inflammatory cells; and (3) attempts at healing by connective tissue replacement of damaged tissue (fibrosis and angiogenesis). The macrophage is the dominant cellular player in chronic inflammation. Activated macrophages secrete TNF, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, and other cytokines that amplify the inflammatory response and promote adaptive immunity. Granulomatous inflammation is a distinctive pattern of chronic inflammation characterized by aggregates of activated macrophages, often with T lymphocytes, and sometimes associated with central necrosis.

subject: Pathology
chapter: Cell Injury, Cell Death, and Adaptations

sections.title: Overview of Cell Injury
sections.content: Cells are constantly exposed to various types of stress and potentially injurious stimuli. Cells can adapt to stress in several ways: they may undergo hypertrophy (increase in size), hyperplasia (increase in number), atrophy (decrease in size and number), or metaplasia (conversion from one cell type to another). If the adaptive capacity is exceeded, or if the external stress is inherently harmful, cell injury develops. Cell injury may be reversible up to a certain point, but if the stimulus persists or is severe enough, the cell suffers irreversible injury and dies. Cell death is one of the most crucial events in the evolution of disease in any tissue or organ. The two principal pathways of cell death are necrosis and apoptosis. Important causes of cell injury include oxygen deprivation (hypoxia, ischemia), physical agents, chemical agents and drugs, infections, immunologic reactions, genetic derangements, and nutritional imbalances.

sections.title: Reversible Cell Injury and Necrosis
sections.content: Cell injury results when cells are stressed so severely that they are no longer able to adapt or when cells are exposed to inherently damaging agents or suffer from intrinsic abnormalities (e.g., in DNA or proteins). Reversible injury is the stage of cell injury at which the deranged function and morphology of the injured cells can return to normal if the injurious stimulus is removed. The hallmarks of reversible injury are reduced oxidative phosphorylation with resultant depletion of energy stores (ATP), and cellular swelling caused by changes in ion concentrations and water influx. Necrosis is a form of cell death in which cellular membranes fall apart, and cellular enzymes leak out and eventually digest the cell. Necrosis often elicits an inflammatory response. Types of necrosis include coagulative necrosis (most common, structure preserved), liquefactive necrosis (brain infarcts, abscesses), caseous necrosis (TB, granulomas), fat necrosis (acute pancreatitis), and fibrinoid necrosis (immune vasculitis).

sections.title: Apoptosis
sections.content: Apoptosis is a pathway of cell death that is induced by a tightly regulated suicide program in which cells destined to die activate enzymes that degrade the cells' own nuclear DNA and nuclear and cytoplasmic proteins. Apoptotic cells break up into fragments, called apoptotic bodies, which contain portions of the cytoplasm and nucleus. The plasma membrane of the apoptotic cell and apoptotic bodies remains intact, but its structure is altered in such a way that these are 'tasty' targets for phagocytes. The dead cell and its fragments are rapidly cleared before the contents have leaked out, and therefore cell death by this pathway does not elicit an inflammatory response in the host. Apoptosis is mediated by the activation of enzymes called caspases. There are two distinct pathways of caspase activation: the mitochondrial (intrinsic) pathway triggered by DNA damage, cytochrome c release, and BAX/BCL-2 imbalance; and the death receptor (extrinsic) pathway triggered by FAS-ligand and TNF binding to surface death receptors.

sections.title: Definitions and Nomenclature of Neoplasia
sections.content: Neoplasia literally means 'new growth.' A neoplasm is an abnormal mass of tissue, the growth of which exceeds and is uncoordinated with that of the normal tissues and persists in the same excessive manner after cessation of the stimuli which evoked the change. Neoplasms may be benign or malignant. Benign tumors are named by attaching the suffix -oma to the cell of origin (e.g., fibroma, adenoma). Malignant tumors of epithelial origin are called carcinomas; those of mesenchymal origin are called sarcomas. A malignant neoplasm that closely resembles the tissue of origin is well-differentiated; one that shows little resemblance is poorly differentiated or undifferentiated (anaplastic). The fundamental differences between benign and malignant tumors relate to their ability to invade locally and spread distantly (metastasize).

sections.title: Hallmarks of Cancer
sections.content: The hallmarks of cancer are a set of functional capabilities acquired during the multistep development of human tumors. The originally described hallmarks include: (1) sustaining proliferative signaling (oncogene activation, e.g., RAS, MYC); (2) evading growth suppressors (tumor suppressor gene loss, e.g., RB, TP53); (3) activating invasion and metastasis (loss of E-cadherin, matrix metalloproteinase activation); (4) enabling replicative immortality (telomerase activation); (5) inducing angiogenesis (VEGF upregulation); and (6) resisting cell death (BCL-2 overexpression, p53 mutation). Revised hallmarks add: deregulating cellular energetics (Warburg effect), avoiding immune destruction, genomic instability as an enabling characteristic, and tumor-promoting inflammation.

sections.title: Molecular Basis of Cancer
sections.content: Carcinogenesis is a multistep process at both the phenotypic and genetic levels. The molecular basis of cancer revolves around four classes of regulatory genes: (1) proto-oncogenes, which promote cell growth (when mutated to oncogenes); (2) tumor suppressor genes, which inhibit cell growth; (3) genes that regulate apoptosis; and (4) genes involved in DNA repair. Proto-oncogenes encode proteins that promote cell growth (growth factors, growth factor receptors, signal transducers, transcription factors, cell cycle regulators). Oncogenes are activated by point mutations (e.g., RAS in codon 12/13), gene amplification (e.g., N-MYC in neuroblastoma, HER2/NEU in breast cancer), or chromosomal translocations (e.g., BCR-ABL in CML: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22); MYC in Burkitt lymphoma t(8;14)). Tumor suppressor genes (anti-oncogenes) follow the Knudson two-hit hypothesis: both alleles must be inactivated. Key tumor suppressors: RB (retinoblastoma, cell cycle gate-keeper), TP53 (guardian of genome, most commonly mutated in human cancers), APC (colorectal cancer), BRCA1/2 (breast/ovarian cancer).
